summary:
The Second Intifada, also known as the Al-Aqsa Intifada, began in September 2000 following the collapse of the [[event:3371|Camp David Summit]] and was conventionally dated to Ariel Sharon's controversial visit to the Temple Mount/Haram al-Sharif compound on September 28. Palestinian protests and clashes with Israeli security forces rapidly escalated into a sustained armed conflict that lasted until approximately 2005, claiming over 3,000 Palestinian and 1,000 Israeli lives.

The Second Intifada was markedly more violent than the [[event:2728|First Intifada]], featuring suicide bombing campaigns against Israeli civilian targets — cafes, buses, markets — that killed hundreds of Israelis and generated intense Israeli military operations in Palestinian cities. Israel reoccupied Palestinian Authority territory in the West Bank during Operation Defensive Shield in 2002 and began construction of a separation barrier. The conflict effectively destroyed the Oslo peace process and deepened mutual distrust between Israeli and Palestinian societies, setting the framework for the troubled Israeli-Palestinian relationship that would persist through the following decades.
